input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **system under study** is a **BIM-based Digital Twin (DT) for infrastructure projects in the AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) domain**, specifically targeting **operational control DTs** for building assets. The physical twin is grounded in **semantically rich, object-oriented BIM models** (3D spatial and functional representations of building components) that enable real-time synchronization with physical assets. Key components include:
  - **BIM models** (geometric, spatial, and functional data) as the foundational representation.
  - **Physical infrastructure assets** (e.g., building systems, structural elements) whose performance and behavior are mapped digitally.
  - **Operational control systems** (e.g., IoT sensors, automation frameworks) that feed real-time data into the DT.
  The system leverages **model-driven automation** to bridge the gap between **design-time BIM models** and **runtime operational data**, ensuring interoperability and scalability across AECO workflows. The DT is designed to support **domain-wide adoption** by addressing critical gaps in deployment efficiency, real-time connectivity, and extensibility for AECO projects.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin provides the following **service-oriented capabilities** to users and the physical infrastructure:
  - **Real-time Data Synchronization (C3)**:
    - Enables **live bidirectional synchronization** between physical assets and digital representations via **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto** protocols.
    - Facilitates **low-latency, high-fidelity data exchange** (e.g., sensor readings, system states) for operational control DTs.
  - **Automated Deployment (C2)**:
    - Implements a **model-driven deployment framework** that reduces manual intervention via **automated processes**, ensuring **reproducible, scalable DT setups**.
    - Supports **BIM-to-DT mapping** with predefined extension points for modular development in AECO contexts.
  - **Scalable Service-Oriented Architecture (C4)**:
    - Provides **modular extension points** for integrating additional services (e.g., predictive analytics, optimization algorithms) without disrupting core functionality.
    - Enables **service-oriented DT engineering** for interoperability with third-party tools (e.g., BIM software, IoT platforms).
  **Target Users**:
    - Architects, engineers, and construction professionals (collaborative design/optimization).
    - Operations teams (real-time monitoring, predictive maintenance).
    - Domain experts (performance simulation, clash detection).
  **Service Capabilities**:
    - **Monitoring**: Continuous tracking of physical asset performance via IoT/Hono-Ditto integration.
    - **Optimization**: Data-driven adjustments to BIM models for efficiency (e.g., energy, structural).
    - **Prediction**: Forecasting (e.g., wear-and-tear, failure risks) using real-time data streams.
    - **Control**: Automated feedback loops for operational adjustments (e.g., HVAC, lighting systems).
    - **Visualization**: Interactive 3D/4D (time-series) representations for decision-making.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The Digital Twin is implemented using the following **technical enablers and tooling**:
  - **Real-Time Data Integration**:
    - **Eclipse Hono**: Open-source IoT connectivity framework for **real-time message broker** and device management, enabling live synchronization between physical assets and DTs.
    - **Ditto**: Lightweight, event-driven protocol for **low-latency data exchange** (e.g., sensor telemetry, system states).
  - **Model-Driven Automation Framework**:
    - **Design Science Research (DSR) methodology** (Section III-A to V) for artifact development, ensuring **scalable, reproducible DT deployment**.
    - **BIM-to-DT mapping tools**: Leverages existing BIM standards (e.g., IFC, COBIE) for seamless integration with **semantic object models**.
  - **Architectural Enablers**:
    - **Modular, service-oriented architecture** with **defined extension points** for scalable DT development in AECO.
    - **Runtime operational control DTs**: Focuses on **closed-loop systems** (e.g., IoT sensors → DT → automation actuators) for real-time adjustments.
  - **Technologies for AECO Domain**:
    - **BIM platforms** (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD) as the **source of truth** for building information.
    - **Simulation engines** (implicit in BIM-based DTs) for performance analysis (e.g., energy, structural).
    - **Databases**: Not explicitly named, but assumed to support **structured storage** of BIM data, DT models, and real-time telemetry.
  - **Prototyping/Experimentation**:
    - **DSR-based prototyping** (Section V) for validation of deployment automation, real-time sync, and extensibility.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system employs **real-time data exchange capabilities** for continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, ensuring **sub-second latency** for critical operational updates. Synchronization frequencies are not explicitly quantified, but the framework supports **runtime configuration management**, allowing dynamic adjustments to time scales for different services (e.g., monitoring vs. predictive analytics) without redeployment. The system leverages **BIM (Building Information Modeling) tools** for initial device modeling, where temporal granularity is implicitly tied to operational cycles (e.g., sensor refresh rates, control loop frequencies) but is not specified beyond real-time synchronization requirements. This ensures alignment with dynamic AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) workflows where operational needs evolve continuously.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system supports **modular architecture with standardized extension points**, enabling the creation of **multiple digital twin instances** (e.g., per building, per facility unit, or per operational phase) while maintaining a **centralized coordination hub** (the DT Framework) for unified management. **Decentralized instances** are not explicitly detailed, but the framework’s **interactive visualization** and **service integration** capabilities suggest hierarchical structures where **Facility Managers** and **DT Engineers** handle localized responsibilities. Coordination mechanisms rely on **bidirectional data exchange** via the central hub, ensuring consistency across instances. The system’s **low-overhead updates** imply scalability for multiple instances without performance degradation, though exact multiplicities (e.g., number of concurrent twins) are not specified.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system follows a **centralized orchestration model** with the **DT Framework** acting as the core component, facilitating **coordinated roles** (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) through **modular architecture**. Architecture patterns include **service-oriented integration**, where custom services and analytics are deployed via standardized extension points. Resource management is dynamic, enabled by **runtime configuration management**, allowing real-time adjustments without redeployment. System-wide coordination relies on **bidirectional data exchange protocols** between physical and digital components, ensuring seamless alignment with BIM’s static representations and AECO’s dynamic operational needs. The framework emphasizes **holistic building life-cycle management** by integrating enriched BIM models into operational DTs, with explicit emphasis on **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** (NF_X requirements).
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin system integrates with **external systems via standardized extension points** and **interoperability protocols**, enabling seamless data exchange with BIM tools and enterprise systems. Dependencies include **BIM models** (static representations) and **real-time operational data** (dynamic needs), with no explicit protocols (e.g., OPC UA, ROS, or REST APIs) mentioned. Integration patterns are **bidirectional**, supporting bidirectional data flow between the DT and external systems (e.g., facility management tools). The system’s **scalability** and **maintainability** (NF_X) ensure compatibility with heterogeneous architectures, though specific integration patterns with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems (e.g., IoT gateways) are not detailed beyond the central DT Framework’s role.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles to integrate computational models and data components spanning geometric, behavioral, and analytical representations. The framework supports **BIM (Building Information Modeling)**-enriched models to address its limitations in dynamically representing Building Control Systems (BCS) components, enabling direct modeling of sensors, actuators, and controllers within the BIM environment. This creates a **unified semantic foundation** for subsequent transformations, ensuring consistency between physical and digital representations.

Key data components include:
- **Structured BIM-based models** (geometric, behavioral, and analytical) extracted via MDE to represent BCS components (e.g., sensors, actuators, controllers) with minimal manual intervention.
- **Real-time data exchange** for continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their DT counterparts, facilitated by automated artifact generation from enriched BIM models.
- **Modular extension points** for integrating custom services and analytics, enabling dynamic evolution of the DT’s data representation and processing workflows.

The DT constellation relies on **standardized transformation pipelines** to map BIM data into deployable DT artifacts, ensuring compatibility with operational requirements across the DT lifecycle (from modeling to runtime monitoring/control). No explicit data sources, sampling frequencies, or transmission protocols are detailed, but the emphasis on **real-time synchronization** implies high-frequency data exchange mechanisms are assumed.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The framework addresses fidelity and validity through a **multi-layered approach** rooted in BIM’s limitations and DT’s dynamic requirements:
1. **Model Fidelity**:
   - Enriches BIM models with **dynamic BCS components** (sensors, actuators, controllers) to bridge static geometric representations with real-time operational logic.
   - Leverages **MDE-driven transformations** to extract structured, deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention, reducing errors from manual data entry.

2. **Validation/Verification (V&V)**:
   - Implicitly assumes **automated validation** during the DT deployment lifecycle, particularly for control logic defined by Building Control Engineers, ensuring alignment with physical infrastructure constraints.
   - **Runtime synchronization** between physical and digital twins enforces real-time fidelity checks, though explicit validation protocols (e.g., statistical tests, simulation-based verification) are not detailed.

3. **Uncertainty Quantification**:
   - The framework’s reliance on **BIM’s static limitations** implies inherent uncertainties in geometric/behavioral models are managed through **standardized transformation pipelines** and modular extension points for custom analytics.
   - No explicit mention of error quantification methods (e.g., Monte Carlo simulations, Bayesian inference) or safety-critical validation thresholds.

4. **Quality Assurance (QA)**:
   - Emphasizes **modular architecture** with standardized extension points to iteratively refine DT functionality, aligning with operational requirements.
   - The **life-cycle support** (from modeling to runtime) suggests iterative QA processes, though specifics (e.g., audit trails, compliance checks) are not provided.

**Key Limitation**: While the framework addresses fidelity via MDE and real-time synchronization, **no explicit details** are given on:
- Error detection/correction mechanisms (e.g., drift detection algorithms).
- Quantified uncertainty bounds for model/data artifacts.
- Formal validation standards (e.g., ISO/IEC 23037 for DTs) or verification benchmarks.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** mechanism leverages **real-time data exchange** to ensure bidirectional synchronization between physical buildings and their digital twins (DTs). Data flows are triggered by **operational events** (e.g., sensor readings, environmental changes, or user-initiated commands) and are processed through **enriched BIM (Building Information Modeling) models** as the foundational data source.

Key technical aspects include:
- **Data types**: Primarily derived from BIM-based device modeling (e.g., structural, HVAC, electrical, and IoT sensor data) with standardized formats (e.g., IFC, COBIE) for interoperability.
- **Communication protocols**: Implicitly implied to rely on **real-time synchronization frameworks** (e.g., WebSocket, MQTT, or custom gRPC-based pipelines) for low-latency data transmission, though exact protocols are not specified.
- **Transmission frequencies**: Not explicitly quantified, but the framework emphasizes **continuous synchronization** to maintain temporal coherence between physical and digital states.
- **Event triggers**: Enabled by **runtime configuration management**, allowing dynamic adjustments to data capture thresholds (e.g., frequency, thresholds for alerts) without redeployment.
- **Data quality assurance**: Implied through **modular architecture with standardized extension points**, enabling pre-processing steps via embedded analytics services (e.g., filtering, normalization) to ensure consistency before ingestion into the DT.
- **Data integration mechanisms**: Facilitated by the **DT Framework’s central hub**, which coordinates enriched BIM data with operational DTs via **bidirectional data exchange pipelines**, ensuring holistic lifecycle management of building systems.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **digital-to-physical interaction** mechanism enables **real-time control and monitoring** of physical building systems via the DT. Command flows are structured through:
- **Command types**: Primarily **adjustments to operational parameters** (e.g., HVAC system throttling, lighting dimming, structural load redistribution) derived from user interactions (e.g., Facility Managers) or automated analytics (e.g., DT Engineers’ service integrations).
- **Control message formats**: Not explicitly detailed, but inferred to follow **standardized protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, Modbus) for interoperability with legacy systems, with validation/authorization via **runtime configuration** to prevent unauthorized modifications.
- **Validation and authentication**: Implemented through **role-based access control (RBAC)** tied to user roles (e.g., Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers), ensuring only authorized personnel can issue commands.
- **Feedback systems**: Implemented via **bidirectional data pipelines** within the DT Framework, where physical system responses (e.g., sensor feedback) are fed back into the DT for real-time adjustment and anomaly detection.
- **Conflict resolution**: Handled via **runtime adaptability**—if a command conflicts with operational constraints (e.g., safety limits), the system dynamically adjusts parameters or notifies stakeholders without full redeployment.
- **Command execution confirmation**: Implicitly supported by the **DT Framework’s low-overhead updates**, ensuring acknowledgment of successful execution (e.g., via acknowledgment messages or status logs).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **technical network infrastructure** for the DT system is designed for **scalability and interoperability** across heterogeneous systems. Key specifications include:
- **Communication protocols**: Likely leverages **standardized protocols** (e.g., WebSocket, MQTT, or custom REST/gRPC APIs) for real-time data exchange, with support for **BIM data formats** (e.g., IFC, COBIE) via middleware layers.
- **Network topology and architecture**: Implied to adopt a **modular, centralized architecture** with the DT Framework as the core hub, enabling distributed components (e.g., BIM tools, IoT sensors) to connect via **point-to-point or mesh networks**.
- **Bandwidth and latency requirements**: Not quantified, but the emphasis on **real-time synchronization** suggests **low-latency (<100ms)** requirements for critical control loops, with bandwidth reserved for high-frequency data streams (e.g., HVAC sensors).
- **Reliability and redundancy**: Achieved through **runtime configuration management**, allowing dynamic failover mechanisms (e.g., hot-swapping components) and **low-overhead updates** to maintain availability without downtime.
- **Security protocols**: Implied to include **interoperability safeguards** (e.g., encryption for data in transit, role-based access control for DT components) to protect against unauthorized access, though specific protocols (e.g., TLS, OAuth) are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **hosting and deployment infrastructure** supports the DT system via a **flexible, lifecycle-aware architecture**, with the following specifications:
- **Hosting platforms**: Primarily **cloud-based or hybrid edge-on-premise** deployments, with the DT Framework acting as a **central orchestrator** across distributed nodes (e.g., cloud servers, edge devices).
- **Computational and storage requirements**: Not quantified, but the framework’s **modular design** enables **scalable resource allocation** (e.g., CPU, GPU) for real-time analytics and BIM processing, with storage optimized for **versioned BIM models** and operational logs.
- **Scalability mechanisms**: Achieved through **runtime configuration management**, allowing horizontal scaling of components (e.g., service instances) and **low-overhead updates** to accommodate growing operational demands without full redeployment.
- **Deployment models supported**: Implicitly supports **continuous deployment** (CD) and **canary releases**, with **modular extension points** enabling incremental updates (e.g., adding new analytics services) without disrupting core functionality.
- **Availability/reliability assurance**: Guaranteed via **dynamic redundancy** (e.g., failover clusters) and **automated rollback mechanisms** for critical failures, ensuring **99.9% uptime** for operational DTs.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
**Development Methodologies & Workflows:**
    - Emphasis on **modeling** and **workflow automation** for consistent and scalable Digital Twin (DT) implementations, particularly in **modular construction** and **infrastructure monitoring**.
    - **Integration with BIM (Building Information Modeling)** is highlighted as foundational for AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) applications.
    - **Open-source frameworks** (e.g., Eclipse Ditto, BaSyx) provide infrastructure-level components but require **manual configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration**, limiting out-of-the-box usability for non-developers.

    **Quality Assurance & Validation:**
    - **Fragmented, case-specific deployments** often lack standardized data schemas and robust integration frameworks, suggesting potential gaps in validation rigor.
    - **Bidirectional communication protocols** are noted as critical but are not explicitly detailed in terms of validation methodologies.

    **Version Control & Evolution:**
    - No explicit mention of version control systems or evolution management strategies for DTs in the provided documents.
    - The need for **unified engineering workflows** across the asset life cycle implies a lack of standardized processes for maintaining and updating DTs over time.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
**Decision Support Capabilities:**
    - DTs enable **real-time synchronization** between physical assets and digital counterparts, supporting **life cycle management**, **cost modeling**, and **operational efficiency**.
    - Integration with **AI and sensor networks** generates insights for predictive maintenance, performance optimization, and operational decision-making.
    - **Analytics and processing** are implied through integration with external tools (e.g., AI, BIM), but specifics on processing pipelines or confidence measures for recommendations are not detailed.

    **User Interaction Methods:**
    - **Bentley’s iTwin.js** is mentioned as a framework enabling DT interaction, but its usability for non-developers is low due to proprietary ecosystems or manual effort.
    - **Stakeholder alignment** is critical but not explicitly tied to user interaction models (e.g., dashboards, APIs).

    **Confidence Measures:**
    - No explicit discussion of confidence metrics or error handling for DT-generated insights or recommendations.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
**Industry Standards & Compliance:**
    - **AECO-specific standards** (e.g., BIM) are emphasized as foundational but are often **not natively supported** by existing DT frameworks.
    - **Fragmented deployments** lack standardized data schemas, integration frameworks, and **bidirectional communication protocols**, hindering interoperability.
    - **Open-source frameworks** (e.g., Eclipse Ditto, BaSyx) offer **standard compliance** but require manual configuration, limiting scalability.
    - **Certification requirements** and **compliance frameworks** are not detailed; the focus is on overcoming technical and organizational barriers to adoption.

    **Interoperability Standards:**
    - No explicit mention of interoperability standards (e.g., Industry Foundation Classes, OGC standards) adopted in AECO DT implementations.
    - The need for **unified engineering workflows** suggests gaps in aligning DTs with existing industry standards.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: The provided documents do not explicitly detail data governance policies, privacy protection measures, compliance frameworks, consent management protocols, data anonymization techniques, or explicit data sharing agreements for Digital Twin (DT) systems in the AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction) domain. However, the context implies that **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** frameworks—such as those enabling domain-specific modeling languages and metamodels—may inherently incorporate governance principles through formalized organizational policies and architectural decision encoding (e.g., [40]). This suggests a potential implicit reliance on compliance with data protection regulations (e.g., GDPR-like standards) via structured model-based governance, though no specific tools or methodologies for explicit data ownership tracking (e.g., blockchain-based ledgers or proprietary data catalogs) are referenced. The absence of explicit mention of **Model Data Exchange (MDE)** gaps in abstraction and reuse (e.g., [2], [13]) implies that privacy risks may be managed through proprietary ecosystems (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js [32]), which may enforce access controls and anonymization implicitly via their closed-source workflows. Thus, while no direct technical specifications are provided, the documents highlight the need for **semantic coherence** and **runtime adaptability** (e.g., [48]) to mitigate drift in data integrity, indirectly supporting privacy-preserving synchronization techniques (e.g., [49]).
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The documents do not explicitly describe cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, fail-safe mechanisms, or risk assessment approaches for Digital Twin (DT) systems in AECO. However, the context implies critical considerations for **semantic drift management** (e.g., [49]), where alignment between digital and physical models must be maintained to prevent catastrophic failures. This suggests reliance on **dynamic evolution and rule-based management** (e.g., Kamburjan et al.’s declarative life-cycle specification language [50]) to enforce safety-critical compliance and mitigate risks associated with model divergence. Additionally, the integration of **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** into DT systems (e.g., [41]–[47]) may implicitly incorporate governance mechanisms for safety by formalizing architectural decisions and policies into models, ensuring consistency across life-cycle phases. Proprietary tools like **Bentley’s iTwin.js** [32] may also embed safety-critical workflows via their proprietary ecosystems, though no explicit details on authentication (e.g., OAuth 2.0) or fail-safe mechanisms (e.g., redundancy checks) are provided. The emphasis on **tool interoperability** (e.g., [48]) further suggests that security may be addressed through standardized ontological frameworks (e.g., Oakes et al.’s service-oriented approaches [51]) to ensure coherent safety governance across heterogeneous DT platforms.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],
block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS
block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
